s1_b1: Faris built a ramp out of plywood. A launch on one side, a gap, a landing on the other.
s1_b2: One goal. Clear the gap. Land clean on the far side.
s1_b3: He starts low. He doesn't make it. He falls into the gap.
s1_b4: So he builds the launch higher. Now he overshoots — flies past the landing, and crashes.
s1_b5: He lowers it a little. Short again.
s1_b6: He decides the wood is the problem. He sands it smooth. He waxes it.
s1_b7: Same height as before. But now he flies further — and overshoots again. The same ramp gave him two different answers.
s1_b8: He raises, lowers, sands, guesses. He is almost out of plywood, and out of patience.
s1_b9: The ramp keeps lying to him. Same setup, different result. He can't see the rule underneath.
s1_b10: But he was never short of wood. He was short of a model.
s1_b11: Every failed run was energy he didn't count. The height he started from — that became his speed at the bottom.
s1_b12: And the rough wood quietly ate some of it — turned it into heat. Wax the ramp, less is eaten, he goes further.
s1_b13: Same height, two answers — because friction was a term he never wrote down.
s1_b14: So before he touches the real ramp again — he tests it somewhere he can see the energy.
s1_b15: Energy Skate Park. He builds his ramp's shape into the track. Turns the energy bars on.
s1_b16: Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before he does.
s1_b17: How fast at the bottom. How high on the far side. Worked out from the energy — start height, minus what friction takes.
s1_b18: Then he presses play. And he watches the bars, not the skater.
s1_b19: After — he explains the gap. If he said this and the bars said that: which lever did he misjudge? The height? The friction?
s1_b20: He adjusts the friction. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all he gets.
s1_b21: His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells him which energy he forgot.
s1_b22: Now he builds the real ramp. Once. And the landing is clean.
s1_b23: The clean landing was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — why did the same ramp give two answers, and which energy did he keep forgetting?
s1_b24: And if all of this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the book that sat still on the shelf, holding energy it hadn't spent? The cart that turned a push into speed? The pendulum that never lost a thing — until something quietly took it?
s1_b25: This ramp was all of them, at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
